summary: A brief abstract outlining how the reference human genome, knowledge of genetic variation, and new genomic technologies enable the systematic exploration of the genetic basis of complex diseases like cancer.

principles.statement: Understanding common mutations in cancer is critical for advancing targeted treatments and drug discovery.
principles.rationale: Identifying these mutations allows researchers to find new drug targets, stratify patients for clinical trials, and analyze drug response data to determine which patient classes will respond to specific therapies.
principles.source_quote: An understanding of the common mutations that can cause distinct human cancers will be critical for identifying new targets for drug discovery, patient stratification for clinical trials, and analysis of drug response data to delineate classes of patients that respond to therapy.

principles.statement: A comprehensive understanding of the cancer genome requires combining multiple investigative approaches for both germline and somatic mutations.
principles.rationale: Germline mutations are best investigated through large-scale case-control or family studies, while somatic alterations require state-of-the-art technologies like high-density oligonucleotide arrays and targeted resequencing. Only by combining these can we fully understand the cancer genome.
principles.source_quote: Combined, these approaches will lead to a better understanding of the cancer genome.

signature_quotes.text: The availability of a reference human genome sequence-an increasingly dense catalog-knowledge of common genetic variation, and new developments in technology present an unprecedented opportunity to systematically explore the genetic basis of complex human diseases such as cancer.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the inflection point in genomics that enables systematic disease research.
